items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning method was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1942. “1958” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A thermostat is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Negative feedback. “Positive feedback” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Ziegler and Nichols published PID tuning method?
items.why: Exact answer: 1942 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
